task:
That seems incorrect.. The level in that area peaks at 0.9uSv/h.
10:08 PM
Searched available tools

The image shows a Safecast map track near the National Museum of Nuclear Science & History in Albuquerque, NM (near Eubank Boulevard SE). Let me pull the actual radiation data for that area.
Query radiation

The actual Safecast database tells a very different story. All 240 measurements in that area (track 8fVdyw, bGeigie-2271, recorded November 2017 by Marco Kaltofen) show values consistently in the range of 0.11–0.16 µSv/h — completely typical background for Albuquerque. The peak in the returned sample is only ~0.162 µSv/h.

So your observation is correct — 0.9 µSv/h would be a significant anomaly and doesn't match what's actually in the database for that location. A few possibilities for what you might be seeing in that map screenshot:

    Color scale mismatch — the cyan/magenta dot colors in the Safecast simple map use a gradient that can be misleading if the scale anchors are set to a different range (e.g. if the scale was calibrated for a higher-radiation dataset like Fukushima data displayed alongside).
    Display unit confusion — if the map is showing CPM rather than µSv/h, and the conversion factor isn't applied, 130 CPM ≈ 0.9 µSv/h with an LND 7318, but the stored values in the DB are already in µSv/h at ~0.14.
    Wrong track overlaid — there may be a different, higher-value track overlapping that same geographic area, possibly from a different detector with a different sensitivity factor.
    Map tile caching artifact — an old render with stale aggregation values.

Could you share which map view you were looking at (Simple Map, the main safecast.org map, or another tool)? And was the 0.9 µSv/h figure shown as a tooltip on a specific dot, or inferred from the color legend? That would help narrow down whether it's a display/rendering bug or a genuine data discrepancy.

That track has 6582 measuremtn.. Please check more
